metadata.subject: travel and tourism
metadata.content: room â€“ a space for sleeping, e.g., We booked a double room for two nights.; double room â€“ a hotel room for two people, e.g., They stayed in a double room near the city centre.; single room â€“ a hotel room for one person, e.g., He prefers a single room when travelling alone.; twinâ€‘bedded â€“ a room with two separate beds, e.g., The twinâ€‘bedded room is ideal for friends.; hostel â€“ inexpensive accommodation for travelers, e.g., We stayed at a youth hostel during our backpacking trip.; guesthouse â€“ a small private lodging, e.g., The guesthouse offered breakfast included.; resort â€“ a place with facilities for recreation, e.g., The seaside resort attracted many tourists.; attraction â€“ a place or event that draws visitors, e.g., The museum is a major attraction.; landmark â€“ a wellâ€‘known building or site, e.g., The Eiffel Tower is a famous landmark.; monument â€“ a structure erected to commemorate, e.g., The war monument stands in the central park.; ruins â€“ remains of ancient buildings, e.g., The city tour included the ruins of the old castle.; cathedral â€“ a large principal church, e.g., The cathedral dominates the skyline.; fountain â€“ a decorative water feature, e.g., Children love playing near the fountain.; bridge â€“ a structure spanning a gap, e.g., We crossed the wooden bridge over the river.; sight â€“ a place worth seeing, e.g., The view from the hill is a breathtaking sight.; sightseeing â€“ the activity of visiting interesting places, e.g., We spent the day sightseeing around the historic district.; traffic â€“ the movement of vehicles, e.g., Heavy traffic caused a delay.; accident â€“ an unexpected event causing damage, e.g., The car accident blocked the lane.; collision â€“ a crash between vehicles, e.g., A multiâ€‘vehicle collision occurred on the motorway.; breakdown â€“ a mechanical failure, e.g., The bus had a breakdown on the highway.; emergency â€“ a sudden urgent situation, e.g., Emergency services arrived quickly.; rescue â€“ to save from danger, e.g., The rescue team helped the stranded hikers.; budget â€“ lowâ€‘cost, e.g., We found a budget accommodation near the beach.; backpacking â€“ traveling with a backpack, e.g., Backpacking across Europe is popular among young people.; trek â€“ a long walk, especially in nature, e.g., They went on a mountain trek.; hike â€“ a walk in the countryside, e.g., We plan to hike the trail tomorrow.; camp â€“ to stay outdoors, e.g., They camped under the stars.; campsite â€“ a place for setting up a tent, e.g., The campsite had basic facilities.; tent â€“ portable shelter, e.g., He pitched a tent near the lake.; reservation â€“ booking a place, e.g., We made a reservation at the hotel.; checkâ€‘in â€“ to register upon arrival, e.g., Please checkâ€‘in at the reception.; checkâ€‘out â€“ to leave and settle bills, e.g., We will checkâ€‘out tomorrow morning.; fully booked â€“ no vacancies left, e.g., The hotel is fully booked for the weekend.; vacancy â€“ an empty place, e.g., There is a vacancy in the shared dormitory.

metadata.subject: sports
metadata.content:
triathlon â€“ a multiâ€‘sport race that includes swimming, cycling and running. Example: "She trained for months before competing in a triathlon."; pitch â€“ the playing field for sports such as football or rugby. Example: "The team practiced on the new pitch yesterday."; record â€“ an official list of the best performances. Example: "He broke the world record in the 100â€‘metre sprint."; volleyball â€“ a team sport where players hit a ball over a net. Example: "They won the volleyball match in straight sets."; cricket â€“ a batâ€‘andâ€‘ball game popular in Commonwealth countries. Example: "The cricket match was postponed due to rain."; football â€“ the sport known as soccer in some countries. Example: "The football league starts next Saturday."; rugby â€“ a contact team sport played with an oval ball. Example: "Rugby requires great physical strength and teamwork."; score â€“ the number of points achieved in a game. Example: "The final score was 3â€‘2 in favour of the home team."; aerobics â€“ a form of exercise that combines rhythmic activity with stretching. Example: "She attends an aerobics class every Monday evening."; walking â€“ moving at a regular pace by putting one foot in front of the other. Example: "Walking is a simple way to stay fit."; badminton â€“ a racket sport played with a shuttlecock. Example: "They played badminton in the school gym."; swimming â€“ moving through water using arms and legs. Example: "He practises swimming for an hour each day."; diving â€“ jumping into water from a height, often performing acrobatics. Example: "The diving competition was held at the Olympic pool."; tournament â€“ a series of contests to determine a champion. Example: "The tennis tournament attracted players from all over the world."; baseball â€“ a batâ€‘andâ€‘ball game popular in the United States. Example: "The baseball season begins in April."; trophy â€“ a cup or other decorative object given as a prize. Example: "The team lifted the championship trophy after a thrilling final."; basketball â€“ a team sport where points are scored by throwing a ball through a hoop. Example: "Basketball games are played on a rectangular court with two hoops."; weightlifting â€“ the sport of lifting heavy weights in specific movements. Example: "Weightlifting requires both strength and technique."; skating rink â€“ an indoor or outdoor area with a smooth ice surface for skating. Example: "Children love to skate at the local skating rink during winter."; victory â€“ the act of winning a competition or battle. Example: "Their victory was celebrated by fans across the city."; BMX cycling â€“ a form of offâ€‘road bicycle racing on a track with jumps. Example: "He entered the BMX cycling competition last weekend."; ski lift â€“ a mechanical device for transporting skiers up a slope. Example: "The ski lift was operating despite the heavy snowfall."; ski/snowboarding run â€“ a designated slope for skiing or snowboarding. Example: "The beginner ski run is marked in green."; boxing match â€“ a contest where two participants fight using fists with gloves. Example: "The boxing match lasted twelve rounds."; windsÂ­urfing â€“ a water sport that combines elements of surfing and sailing. Example: "She learned windsÂ­urfing during her vacation on the coast."; canoeing â€“ paddling a narrow boat with a single-bladed paddle. Example: "Canoeing on the lake is a relaxing weekend activity."; yoga â€“ a system of physical postures and breath control for health and relaxation. Example: "He practices yoga every morning to improve flexibility."; stadium â€“ a large structure with a field for sports and spectators. Example: "The final will be held in the national stadium."; medal â€“ an award for first, second or third place in a competition. Example: "She received a gold medal for her performance in the swim meet."; track â€“ a prepared surface for running races. Example: "The athletes warmed up on the track before the 400â€‘metre race."; own goal â€“ a goal scored accidentally against one's own team. Example: "The defender's mistake resulted in an own goal for the opposition."; chess â€“ a board game of strategy played between two players. Example: "He participated in the school chess tournament."; running â€“ moving quickly on foot, usually for exercise or competition. Example: "She goes running every evening to stay fit."; climbing â€“ the activity of ascending natural or artificial surfaces. Example: "Rock climbing requires both strength and mental focus."; halfâ€‘marathon â€“ a road running competition of 21.0975 kilometres. Example: "She completed her first halfâ€‘marathon last month."; marathon â€“ a longâ€‘distance running race of 42.195 kilometres. Example: "Training for a marathon takes many months of dedicated workouts."; sprint â€“ a short, highâ€‘speed race. Example: "The 100â€‘metre sprint is often the most exciting event in athletics."; medalâ€‘winning â€“ achieving a place that earns a medal. Example: "Her medalâ€‘winning performance set a new national record."
